title_en: Introduction: The Obligation of Knowing Allah, Islam, and the Prophet
title_id: Pendahuluan: Kewajiban Mengenal Allah, Islam, dan Nabi
content_id:
Imam Muhammad ibn Abdul Wahhab membuka teks fundamental ini dengan masalah yang sangat mendesak: bahwa Allah telah mewajibkan setiap Muslim — laki-laki dan perempuan — untuk mengetahui tiga hal dengan pasti. Ini adalah pengetahuan tentang Tuhannya (Allah), pengetahuan tentang agamanya (Islam), dan pengetahuan tentang Nabinya (Muhammad, shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam). Ini bukan latihan intelektual yang bersifat opsional tetapi kewajiban agama yang pengabaiannya membawa konsekuensi serius.

Pengarang memulai dengan menyebut rahmat dan berkah Allah kemudian menetapkan kerangka seluruh risalah. Beliau menarik perhatian pada fakta bahwa setiap Muslim akan ditanya tentang tiga hal ini di kubur. Malaikat Munkar dan Nakir akan menanyai almarhum: 'Siapakah Tuhanmu? Apa agamamu? Siapakah nabimu?' Kemampuan untuk menjawab pertanyaan-pertanyaan ini dengan keyakinan yang teguh — berakar pada pengetahuan, bukan sekadar kebiasaan atau identitas budaya — adalah buah dari pembelajaran yang benar dan keimanan yang tulus.

Ibn Abdul Wahhab mendasarkan kewajiban ini pada Alquran dan Sunnah. Allah berfirman: 'Maka ketahuilah bahwa tidak ada tuhan selain Allah' (Muhammad, 47:19). Ayat ini menggunakan kata perintah 'ketahuilah' (fa'lam), menjadikan pengetahuan tentang tauhid sebagai perintah, bukan saran. Demikian pula, Nabi, shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam, bersabda: 'Mencari ilmu adalah kewajiban bagi setiap Muslim' (Ibn Majah). Para ulama Islam telah memahami dari teks-teks seperti ini bahwa pengetahuan yang paling wajib adalah pengetahuan tentang fundamental-fundamental keimanan.

Pendahuluan ini juga membawa kepedulian pastoral yang mendalam. Pengarang menulis pada saat kebodohan yang meluas telah merayap ke dalam komunitas-komunitas Muslim, dan praktik-praktik yang tidak sesuai dengan tauhid murni telah menjadi hal yang biasa. Tujuannya bukan untuk menciptakan elit ilmiah tetapi untuk membawa setiap Muslim kembali ke hal-hal dasar — tiga pertanyaan yang akan dihadapi setiap jiwa, terdidik atau tidak, setelah kematian.

Pengarang juga secara singkat memperkenalkan konsep bukti (dalil). Pengetahuan sejati dalam Islam tidak didasarkan pada tradisi buta atau warisan budaya. Pengetahuan harus berakar pada dalil-dalil dari Alquran dan Sunnah yang sahih. Di seluruh risalah, Ibn Abdul Wahhab secara konsisten memasangkan setiap prinsip dengan bukti tekstualnya, melatih pembaca untuk berpikir dalam hal 'Apa dalilnya?' Ini adalah ciri khas metodologi Salafi yang beliau warisi dari para ulama klasik.

Akhirnya, pendahuluan menetapkan nada buku: mudah diakses, langsung, dan dibangun untuk Muslim biasa. Tidak seperti risalah-risalah teologis yang padat yang ditujukan kepada spesialis, Al-Usul ath-Thalathah dirancang untuk dihafal dan dipahami oleh mukmin biasa. Keringkasannya adalah disengaja. Pengarang ingin setiap rumah tangga Muslim menginternalisasi prinsip-prinsip ini, sehingga ketika kubur tertutup dan para malaikat bertanya, si mukmin menjawab dengan kejelasan, keyakinan, dan cahaya pengetahuan daripada kegelapan kebodohan.
content_en:
Imam Muhammad ibn Abdul Wahhab opens this foundational text with a matter of supreme urgency: that Allah has made it obligatory upon every Muslim — man and woman — to know three things with certainty. These are knowledge of one's Lord (Allah), knowledge of one's religion (Islam), and knowledge of one's Prophet (Muhammad, peace be upon him). This is not an optional intellectual exercise but a religious duty whose neglect carries serious consequence.

The author begins by invoking the mercy and blessings of Allah and then establishes the framework of the entire treatise. He draws attention to the fact that every Muslim will be asked about these three things in the grave. The angels Munkar and Nakir will question the deceased: "Who is your Lord? What is your religion? Who is your prophet?" The ability to answer these questions with firm conviction — rooted in knowledge, not mere habit or cultural identity — is the fruit of proper learning and sincere faith.

Ibn Abdul Wahhab grounds this obligation in the Quran and Sunnah. Allah says: "So know that there is no deity except Allah" (Muhammad, 47:19). This verse uses the imperative "know" (fa'lam), making knowledge of tawhid a command, not a suggestion. Similarly, the Prophet, peace be upon him, said: "Seeking knowledge is an obligation upon every Muslim" (Ibn Majah). The scholars of Islam have understood from such texts that the most obligatory knowledge is knowledge of the fundamentals of the faith.

The introduction also carries a profound pastoral concern. The author was writing in a time when widespread ignorance had crept into Muslim communities, and practices inconsistent with pure tawhid had become normalized. His goal was not to create a scholarly elite but to bring every Muslim back to the basics — the three questions that every soul, educated or not, will face.

The author also briefly introduces the concept of evidence (dalil). True knowledge in Islam is not based on blind tradition or cultural inheritance. It must be grounded in proofs from the Quran and authentic Sunnah. Throughout the treatise, Ibn Abdul Wahhab consistently pairs each principle with its textual evidence, training the reader to think in terms of "What is the proof?" This is a hallmark of the Salafi methodology he inherited from the classical scholars.

Finally, the introduction sets the tone of the book: accessible, direct, and built for the common Muslim. Unlike dense theological treatises aimed at specialists, Al-Usul ath-Thalathah is designed to be memorized and understood by ordinary believers. Its brevity is intentional. The author wanted every Muslim household to have these principles internalized, so that when the grave closes and the angels ask, the believer answers with clarity, conviction, and the light of knowledge rather than the darkness of ignorance.
content_ar: الإمام محمد ﷺ ibn Abdul Wahhab opens this foundational text with a matter of supreme urgency: that الله has made it obligatory upon every مسلم — man and woman — لمعرفة three things with certainty. These are العلم of one's Lord (الله), العلم of one's religion (الإسلام), and العلم of one's النبي (محمد ﷺ, ﷺ). This is not an optional intellectual exercise but a religious الواجب whose neglect carries serious consequence. The author begins by invoking the الرحمة and البركات of الله and then establishes the framework of the entire treatise. He draws attention to the fact that every مسلم will be asked about these three things in the القبر. The الملائكة Munkar and Nakir will question the deceased: "Who is your Lord? What is your religion? Who is your النبي?" The ability to answer these questions with firm conviction — rooted in العلم, not mere habit or cultural identity — is the fruit of proper learning and sincere الإيمان. Ibn Abdul Wahhab grounds this الالتزام in the القرآن الكريم and السنة. الله says: "لذا know that there is no deity except الله" (محمد ﷺ, 47:19). This الآية uses the imperative "know" (fa'lam), making العلم of tawhid a command, not a suggestion. Similarly, النبي ﷺ, ﷺ, said: "Seeking العلم is an الالتزام upon every مسلم" (ابن ماجه). The العلماء of الإسلام have understood from such texts that the most obligatory العلم is العلم of the fundamentals of the الإيمان. The introduction أيضاً carries a profound pastoral concern. The author was writing in a time عندما widespread ignorance had crept into مسلم communities, and practices inconsistent with pure tawhid had become normalized. His goal was not to create a العلمي elite but to bring every مسلم back to the basics — the three questions that every النفس, educated or not, will face. The author أيضاً briefly introduces the concept of evidence (dalil). True العلم in الإسلام is not based on blind التراث or cultural inheritance. It must be grounded in proofs from the القرآن الكريم and authentic السنة. Throughout the treatise, Ibn Abdul Wahhab consistently pairs each principle with its textual evidence, training the reader to think in terms of "What is the proof?" This is a hallmark of the السلفي methodology he inherited from the الكلاسيكي العلماء. وأخيراً, the introduction sets the tone of the الكتاب: accessible, direct, and built for the common مسلم. Unlike dense theological treatises aimed at specialists, Al-الأصول ath-Thalathah is designed to be memorized and understood by ordinary المؤمنون. Its brevity is intentional. The author wanted every مسلم household to have these principles internalized, لذا that عندما the القبر closes and the الملائكة ask, the المؤمن answers with clarity, conviction, and the light of العلم rather than the darkness of ignorance.
